//: Hand-edited corrections applied to the Bedrock-sourced entity_models.json / entity_geometry.json. Loaded by EntityModelLoader per entity; keys are `geometry_ref`, `texture_ref`, `inventory_y_rotation`, `bone_overrides`, `hidden_bones`. `hidden_bones` strips bones Bedrock packs into the shared geometry but Java conditionally hides at render time (saddlebags on plain horses, saddles on undead mounts).

entities.//minecraft:skeleton_horse: Bedrock authors the skeleton horse leg cubes with the bone silhouette texture pushed onto faces that don't face the camera in our standard iso pose. Front-right Leg3 (X=+4, Z=-8) needs -90 yaw and front-left Leg4 (X=-4, Z=-8) needs +90 yaw to swing their bone-face into the iso-visible set; rear legs Leg1/Leg2 (Z=11) both need 180 yaw. Each leg bone has a single cube and no parent, so bone-level rotation with no explicit pivot rotates the cube about its own bbox center (the default introduced 2026-04-26).
entities.//minecraft:blaze: Bedrock geometry.blaze authors 12 upperBodyParts0..11 bones all stacked at the same pivot (0, 24, 0) with identical cube origins (0, 16, 0); Java BlazeModel.createBodyLayer distributes them via PartPose.offset across three rings of four rods each. Outer ring (parts 0..3) at radius 9 sits above the head pivot (Java jy=-2+cos(i*0.5)); middle ring (parts 4..7) at radius 7 just below (jy=2+cos(i*0.5), angles offset by pi/4); inner ring (parts 8..11) at radius 5 well below the head (jy=11+cos(i*0.75), angles offset by 3*pi/20). Java Y points down in model space so Bedrock dy = -jy. The bind-pose scraper only captures rotations from PartPose.offsetAndRotation, not pure translations, so the offsets don't reach entity_bind_poses.json. We materialise the 12 rod positions here as extra_bones templated from upperBodyParts0, then hide the 12 stacked originals.
entities.//minecraft:sheep: Vanilla Java composes a normal sheep from two layers - SheepModel renders the actual sheep body (full long legs, snouted head, tan skin) and SheepFurModel overlays the white wool covering on top (wool body + short leg-stubs + small head, no snout). Bedrock ships these as separate geometries: geometry.sheep.sheared.v1.8 is the skin and geometry.sheep.v1.8 is the wool. With only the wool geometry assigned to minecraft:sheep, the actual sheep body underneath is invisible. Re-root the entity at the sheared geometry so the skin is the base layer, then overlay the wool. Both share the sheep/sheep texture which packs the skin UVs in the top half and the wool UVs in the bottom half. Texture partial-alpha is bumped to opaque at tooling time via OPAQUE_ALPHA_TEXTURE_REFS.
entities.//minecraft:slime: Vanilla Java SlimeModel renders two stacked cubes: a 6x6x6 inner body and an 8x8x8 outer shell drawn translucent (entity/slime/slime.png with alpha=180 in the outer-shell UV strip at [0, 0]). Bedrock's geometry.slime only ships the inner cube + eyes/mouth - the outer shell isn't a separate Bedrock bone, just a UV strip on the same texture that vanilla Java composes via SlimeOuterLayer. Synthesize the outer cube as an extra bone templated from the inner cube. The outer-shell strip on slime.png is authored at 8-pixel face dimensions (TOP+BOTTOM occupy u 8..24 v 0..8, sides occupy u 0..32 v 8..16), so the override sets size=[8, 8, 8] and origin_offset=[-1, -1, -1] explicitly rather than templating from the inner's 6x6x6 with inflate - the strip-unwrap formula keys off size, not size+inflate, and a 6-sized strip would sample empty pixels. Texture's authored alpha=180 in that region renders as proper translucency without any per-overlay alpha flag.
entities.//minecraft:armadillo: Bedrock geometry.armadillo ships both 'body' (standing pose) and 'body_rolled_up' (defensive ball pose) bones; Java's ArmadilloModel toggles visibility per entity state at runtime. Without animation state we default to the standing pose; hide the rolled-up bone.
entities.//minecraft:guardian: Bedrock geometry.guardian.v1.8 stacks all 12 'spikepart0..11' bones at the same pivot (0, 24, 0) with identical cubes; Java's GuardianModel distributes them at runtime to the 12 edge midpoints of the body cube (16x16x16 spanning Bedrock X/Z [-8, +8] and Y [0, 16]) via SPIKE_X/SPIKE_Y/SPIKE_Z arrays plus per-spike rotations from SPIKE_X_ROT/Y_ROT/Z_ROT (each multiplied by pi). Y axis is flipped between Java (Y-down) and Bedrock (Y-up), so Bedrock cube_offset Y = -16 - SPIKE_Y[i] (which lands the spike pivot at body Y = 8 - SPIKE_Y[i], with the body Y midpoint at 8). Pitch axis is also flipped so Bedrock pitch = -SPIKE_X_ROT*180; same flip on yaw and roll. Eye cube was authored at z=0 (inside body); Java's createBodyLayer offsets it z=-8.25 so it sits on the body's front face - mirror that with cube_offset.
entities.//minecraft:elder_guardian: Same geometry as guardian (geometry.guardian.v1.8) so reuse all the spike/eye overrides. Vanilla Java has a separate guardian_elder.png texture (paler, larger looking variant) that the Bedrock pack doesn't ship; bundled from the Java client jar via FORCED_EXTRA_TEXTURE_REFS. The override texture_ref points at it.
entities.//minecraft:vindicator: Bedrock geometry.vindicator.v1.8 ships both arm sets - 'arms' folded across the chest (idle pose) plus 'rightArm'/'leftArm' hanging straight down (aggressive pose). Java's IllagerModel toggles visibility per entity state at runtime. Without animation state we default to the folded idle pose; hide the hanging pair so only crossed arms render.
entities.//minecraft:evoker: Same illager rig as vindicator - both arm sets ship together. Hide the hanging pair so only the folded idle pose renders.
entities.//minecraft:copper_golem_flower: Bedrock authors geometry.copper_golem.flower as a thin overlay carrying just the right_arm holding the flower (no body, head, or legs); vanilla Java composes the full golem by rendering the base mesh first and the flower mesh on top. Re-root the entity's base at geometry.copper_golem so the auto-fit anchors on the full body silhouette (matching the plain copper_golem icon size), then layer the flower-only geometry on top with its own texture. Same coordinate frame on both geometries means no per-overlay placement is needed.
entities.//minecraft:creeper_charged: Vanilla Java draws the standard creeper mesh and texture, then a translucent inflated 'power' overlay using entity/creeper/creeper_armor.png - that lightning-grid texture is what makes a charged creeper visually distinct from a plain creeper. Bedrock packs the inflated geometry as geometry.creeper.charged.v1.8 so the variant ships with the right mesh; this override re-roots the entity's base layer at the plain creeper geometry/texture and adds the charged geometry with the creeper_armor texture as an overlay. The creeper_armor.png ref must be present in entity_textures/creeper/ - if it's missing (the texture isn't bundled by ToolingEntityModels until the user runs ./gradlew entityModels with the forced-extra-texture list) the overlay drops silently and the renderer degrades to a plain creeper.
entities.//minecraft:snow_golem: Bedrock arm1/arm2 share identical cube geometry (origin [1,20,-1] size [12,2,2]) and rely on the animation system to rotate arm2 around yaw 180 for the opposite stick. With no animations here both arms stack in +X. Java SnowGolemModel's bind pose rotates the arms around Z by ~57.3 degrees; we mirror that here. arm2 also takes yaw 180 so it extends in -X instead of +X. Explicit pivot is the arm's bone pivot at the body shoulder so the rotations articulate at the joint (the default cube-center anchor would not flip arm2 to the body's other side and would not roll arm1 outward from the shoulder).
entities.//minecraft:spider: Vanilla Java draws spider eyes as a separate full-bright additive layer on top of the body via SpiderEyesLayer + RenderType.eyes. Bedrock bakes the eye glow into the body texture as alpha=3 spots (currently bumped to opaque via OPAQUE_ALPHA_TEXTURE_REFS as a workaround). With emissive overlay support landed, layer the Java spider_eyes.png on top of the same geometry with emissive: true so the eyes glow correctly without depending on the alpha bump. The overlay reuses geometry.spider.v1.8 so eye UVs land on the head face; the rest of the overlay samples transparent pixels from the eye-only PNG and renders nothing.
entities.//minecraft:cave_spider: Same emissive eye overlay as spider - vanilla Java's CaveSpiderRenderer reuses SpiderEyesLayer with the same spider_eyes.png (the cave-spider blue tint is baked into the body texture, not the eye texture). Reuses spider/spider_eyes.
entities.//minecraft:fox: Bedrock fox body is authored vertically and laid horizontal via a Java-side 90 deg pitch bind pose. bindPoseRotation in this renderer only affects the bone's own cubes - tail (child of body) stays vertical and looks 90 deg off. Override tail with its own 90 deg pitch to follow the body. Explicit pivot is the tail's bone pivot at the body-tail joint (the default cube-center anchor would rotate the tail about its midpoint instead of swinging from its base).
entities.//minecraft:wolf: Bedrock authors upperBody (the mane/scruff) at pivot (-1, 10, 2) with cube Z=[-1, 6] - the back of the body. Java's upper_body bind pose anchors at pivot (-1, 14, -3) - 5 units forward, at the neck/head junction. applyBindPoses sets only the rotation, not the pivot, so our 90 deg pitch rotates around Bedrock's back-pivot and leaves the mane visually too far back. Shift the cube +5 forward in Z so the resulting pivot lands at Z=-3 (matching Java's anchor) and the bind-pose rotation hinges from the correct point.
entities.//minecraft:enderman: Bedrock geometry.enderman.v1.8 authors the head cube at Y=[24,32], overlapping the bottom of the body (Y=[26,38]) - the head sits inside the chest. The hat (child of head) is correctly positioned at Y=[38,45], at the top of the body where the head should be. Java's EndermanModel places the head pivot at the top of the body (Y=38). Shift the head cube +14 in Y so it lands above the body (Y=[38,46]) and aligns with the already-correct hat.
entities.//minecraft:chicken: No override needed: Bedrock authors leg0/leg1 (3x5x3 cubes at uv 26, 0) with the leg silhouette on the SOUTH face (col 36, rows 3-7) and the foot bottom on the DOWN face (cols 32-34, rows 0-2). Both are back-facing in our standard iso pose [pitch=30, yaw=225]. EntityGeometryKit.shouldCullBackFaces auto-detects this case: cubes whose iso-visible faces (TOP/NORTH/EAST) have empty UV rects but whose iso-hidden faces (DOWN/SOUTH/WEST) have content opt out of back-face culling, so the back faces render at their natural cube positions - foot at the cube's bottom, silhouette on the back face. Same applies to chicken_cold (same geometry layout).
entities.//minecraft:ender_dragon: Bedrock dragon.geo authors duplicated right-side parts (wing1, wingtip1, rearlegtip1, rearfoot1, frontlegtip1, frontfoot1) with cube origins identical to their left siblings, relying on Molang animations to translate the mirror into place. Tips and feet are also authored X-centred (origin X symmetric about 0) relying on Molang to slot them under each parent leg. We fill the gap with cube_offset shifts that land each duplicate under the correct leg column and mirror wingtip1 to the +X side.

minecraft:ender_dragon.//head_neck_y_shift: Bedrock authoring places head/neck bone cubes at Y=[16,32]/[19,29] centered at Y=24, well above the body's Y=[-4,20] center 8. Java's authoritative layout has head in FRONT of the body at similar Y, not above it. Corrective shift Y=-16 brings head/neck cubes down to the body's Y range so the head/neck plug into the body front rather than perching on top.
minecraft:ender_dragon.//legs_note: Java EnderDragonModel applies pitch rotations in a leg→tip→foot hierarchy: frontleg 1.3 rad (74.48 deg), frontlegtip -0.5 rad (-28.65) relative, frontfoot 0.75 rad (42.97) relative. Rear: leg 1.0 rad (57.30), tip 0.5 rad (28.65), foot 0.75 rad (42.97). Bedrock authors tips/feet as root-level bones with cubes at X=0, relying on the animation system to chain them. We reparent each tip to its leg and each foot to its tip so the rotation chain propagates, then shift cubes so each part attaches at the end of its parent. Each rotation uses an explicit `pivot` at the bone's authored joint anchor - without it, the new default would rotate about each cube's own center and break joint articulation. Rotations use Bedrock sign convention (+pitch = forward tilt = top-of-bone moves toward -Z).
minecraft:ender_dragon.//extra_bones: Dragon tail: Java EnderDragonModel builds 12 tail segments at PartPose.offset(0, 10, 60 + i*10) as copies of the shared neck cube. Templated from the (post-override) neck which is already Y-shifted down by -16, so the tail inherits the corrected Y position without a separate Y shift here.
minecraft:ender_dragon.//emissive_eyes: Vanilla Java draws ender dragon eyes as a separate full-bright additive layer via EnderDragonRenderer.DragonEyes + RenderType.eyes. Layer the Java enderdragon/dragon_eyes.png on top of the same geometry with emissive: true so the eyes glow correctly. The overlay reuses geometry.dragon so eye UVs land on the head face; non-eye regions sample transparent pixels from the eye-only PNG and render nothing.
